This report analyzes 50,000 container movements over 180 days. The average container dwell time is 4.9 days (median: 3.0 days), with 22.0% of containers exceeding the overstay threshold.

How to read this report
This report is structured as a diagnostic tool for your terminal's yard operations. Sections 1–4 describe what is happening (data quality, dwell patterns, yard load). Sections 5–6 apply analytical models to segment container behaviour and forecast future utilization. Section 7 translates findings into dollar-value business impact. Section 8 provides specific, actionable recommendations per container segment.

What this means
Data quality checks ensure the analytics pipeline is working with clean, reliable records. All records passed validation with no exclusions — this is an ideal dataset. 3.2% of containers have no gate-out timestamp — these are containers currently still in the yard and are excluded from dwell time calculations but included in utilization analysis.

3. Dwell Time Distribution Analysis

Reading the histogram
The histogram shows how container dwell times are distributed. The tall bars on the left represent the majority of containers that move through the terminal within 1–5 days. The median (3.0 days) is lower than the mean (4.9 days) because a small number of long-dwelling containers pull the average up — this right-skewed "long tail" is typical of port operations and represents customs delays, documentation disputes, and abandoned cargo.
How to read the cumulative curve
This chart answers the most important operational question: “What percentage of containers have left the yard by day X?” Each red dot marks a key milestone. For example, 50% of containers depart by day 3.0, and 90% depart by day 8.4. The remaining 10% — the long tail above the 90% line — are the containers that consume disproportionate yard capacity and drive congestion. 10,658 containers exceed the overstay threshold, and 720 have dwelled more than 30 days.
Dwell patterns by movement type
Each panel shows one movement type's dwell distribution separately, so you can compare shapes without overlap. The red dashed line marks the median. The stats box in each panel shows count, median, mean, and 95th percentile. Import containers typically dwell longest because they depend on consignee pickup timing. Exports are tighter because they're time-bound to vessel departure schedules. Transhipment has the shortest tail — these move vessel-to-vessel on fixed schedules.
Container type comparison
Each bar shows the average dwell time for that container type. The annotations show median dwell, overstay percentage, and container count. Types with higher overstay rates are consuming disproportionate yard capacity and should be targeted for clearance improvement.
  • 95% of all containers leave within 14.5 days. The remaining 5% are the long tail driving congestion.
  • Flow path impact: Containers on the vessel→rail path average 6.6 days dwell, while vessel→vessel averages 4.2 days. Rail-bound containers dwell longer because rail departure schedules are less frequent than truck pickups. Consider coordinating additional rail services to reduce this gap.
  • Shipping line variation: HMM containers average 6.0 days vs Maersk at 4.4 days. Engage slower lines about improving pickup performance.

Dwell Category Breakdown

CategoryCountPercentage
normal17,17235.5%
long13,62028.1%
overstay10,65822.0%
short6,96814.4%
Dwell categories explained
Short (<24h): Transhipment or pre-cleared imports moving through rapidly. Normal (1–3 days): Standard operations within free period. Long (3–7 days): Approaching or at free-day limit; may start incurring charges. Overstay (>7 days): Beyond the threshold; generating demurrage revenue but consuming yard capacity.

4. Yard Utilization Analysis

Reading the heatmap
Each row is a yard block (busiest at top); each column is a week. Green = low utilization (plenty of space), yellow = moderate (40–65%), orange/red = high utilization (65%+) (approaching capacity). Industry benchmark: sustained utilization above 75–80% causes operational congestion (more reshuffles, slower crane operations, gate delays). Look for blocks that are consistently yellow/orange — these are your chronic congestion risks. Blocks that are consistently dark green may be candidates for reallocation.

Block-level findings
  • No blocks exceeded the 75% congestion threshold — yard capacity is currently healthy.
  • Blocks E01, H01, R01 are under-utilized (avg <30%). These may be specialty blocks (hazardous, reefer) with lower volume by design, or candidates for reallocation.

5. Segmentation Results

Best model: KMeans with k=8 segments. Silhouette score: 0.3709 (Weak but usable)

Understanding the clustering charts
  • Elbow Plot: Shows how adding more clusters reduces within-cluster variation. The "elbow" (where the curve bends) suggests the natural number of groups in the data.
  • Silhouette Score: Measures how well-separated the clusters are (0 to 1). Higher is better. Scores above 0.5 indicate clear, distinct groups. The curve shows scores for k=2 through k=10 for both K-Means and Hierarchical.
  • Dendrogram: The hierarchical clustering tree shows how containers merge into groups from bottom to top. The red dashed line marks where we "cut" the tree to get our chosen number of segments. Tall vertical lines before a merge indicate well-separated groups.
  • Centroid Comparison: This is the key interpretability chart — it shows what makes each segment different. Each cluster of bars represents one feature (dwell, TEU, cost, weight). Segments with tall bars on “Dwell Hours” but short bars on “TEU” are defined by long dwell, not container size. This is the unsupervised learning equivalent of feature importance.
Why data-driven segmentation instead of fixed rules?
You could classify containers with fixed rules (e.g., “under 3 days = fast, over 7 = overstay”). However, fixed rules are arbitrary — the right threshold depends on your terminal's specific patterns. K-Means clustering lets the data reveal natural groupings based on actual dwell time, container size (TEU), and storage cost together. The algorithm finds where the real breaks are in your data, not where a textbook says they should be. This means the segments are specific to your terminal's operations and will shift as your operations change over time.

Segment-by-Segment Inference

Fast Movers — 4,062 containers (8.4% of total)
Who: Primarily dry containers (83%), mainly import (43%) movement, most common flow path: vessel→truck.
Behavior: Average dwell 0.5 days (median 0.5), 0% overstaying, avg storage cost $3 per container.
Why: Transhipment cargo or pre-cleared imports with efficient pickup logistics.
Action: These are your best performers. Protect their fast-track lane; optimize gate scheduling to minimize their wait time. Any increase in this segment's size means your terminal is getting more efficient.
Efficient Operators (20ft) — 9,702 containers (20.0% of total)
Who: Primarily dry containers (82%), mainly export (43%) movement, most common flow path: truck→vessel.
Behavior: Average dwell 2.2 days (median 2.3), 2% overstaying, avg storage cost $1 per container.
Why: Well-coordinated supply chains with timely customs clearance and pickup.
Action: The ideal segment to grow. Consider loyalty incentives for shipping lines that consistently land here. These containers generate throughput revenue without clogging yard space.
Efficient Operators (40ft, mid-range) — 6,928 containers (14.3% of total)
Who: Primarily dry containers (79%), mainly export (47%) movement, most common flow path: truck→vessel.
Behavior: Average dwell 2.6 days (median 2.5), 3% overstaying, avg storage cost $0 per container.
Why: Well-coordinated supply chains with timely customs clearance and pickup.
Action: The ideal segment to grow. Consider loyalty incentives for shipping lines that consistently land here. These containers generate throughput revenue without clogging yard space.
Efficient Operators (40ft, longest dwell) — 7,941 containers (16.4% of total)
Who: Primarily dry containers (98%), mainly export (45%) movement, most common flow path: truck→vessel.
Behavior: Average dwell 2.8 days (median 2.6), 4% overstaying, avg storage cost $0 per container.
Why: Well-coordinated supply chains with timely customs clearance and pickup.
Action: The ideal segment to grow. Consider loyalty incentives for shipping lines that consistently land here. These containers generate throughput revenue without clogging yard space.
Standard Dwellers (40ft) — 5,107 containers (10.5% of total)
Who: Primarily dry containers (57%), mainly import (73%) movement, most common flow path: vessel→truck.
Behavior: Average dwell 4.8 days (median 4.4), 40% overstaying, avg storage cost $95 per container.
Why: Typical operations approaching or at the free-day boundary. Some delays from documentation or customs processing.
Action: Monitor for drift toward Extended Stay. Automated pickup reminders at day 3–4 can nudge containers out before they cross into penalty territory. Small improvements here have large aggregate impact due to segment size.
Standard Dwellers (40ft, mid-range) — 5,909 containers (12.2% of total)
Who: Primarily dry containers (68%), mainly import (68%) movement, most common flow path: vessel→truck.
Behavior: Average dwell 5.9 days (median 4.7), 45% overstaying, avg storage cost $164 per container.
Why: Typical operations approaching or at the free-day boundary. Some delays from documentation or customs processing.
Action: Monitor for drift toward Extended Stay. Automated pickup reminders at day 3–4 can nudge containers out before they cross into penalty territory. Small improvements here have large aggregate impact due to segment size.
Standard Dwellers (20ft, longest dwell) — 6,586 containers (13.6% of total)
Who: Primarily dry containers (54%), mainly import (65%) movement, most common flow path: vessel→truck.
Behavior: Average dwell 6.2 days (median 4.8), 46% overstaying, avg storage cost $96 per container.
Why: Typical operations approaching or at the free-day boundary. Some delays from documentation or customs processing.
Action: Monitor for drift toward Extended Stay. Automated pickup reminders at day 3–4 can nudge containers out before they cross into penalty territory. Small improvements here have large aggregate impact due to segment size.
Chronic Blockers — 2,183 containers (4.5% of total)
Who: Primarily dry containers (63%), mainly import (52%) movement, most common flow path: vessel→truck.
Behavior: Average dwell 34.4 days (median 19.4), 100% overstaying, avg storage cost $3,077 per container.
Why: Almost certainly abandoned cargo, unclaimed empties awaiting repositioning orders, or containers in legal disputes.
Action: URGENT — initiate formal clearance procedures. Assess legal options for disposal of abandoned cargo. Relocate immediately to peripheral storage. Each container in this segment has been consuming a TEU-slot for 34+ days — that's 34 days of lost throughput capacity per slot.
Key takeaway for terminal management
The segmentation reveals that your containers are NOT one homogeneous group — they have distinct behavior patterns requiring different management strategies. A blanket “reduce dwell time” policy misses the nuance. Instead, target each segment with the specific action described above. The highest ROI comes from converting Extended Stay containers into Standard Dwellers (reduce by 3–5 days) and clearing Overstayers and Chronic Blockers to free yard capacity for additional throughput.

6. Forecasting Results

Model Comparison

StatsModels_HoltWinters15.34%598.39499.87

Selected model: HoltWinters_Additive (forecast horizon: 14 days)

Understanding the forecast
  • MAPE (Mean Absolute Percentage Error) measures forecast accuracy. Under 10% = excellent accuracy. Your forecast is highly reliable.
  • The shaded band around the forecast line represents the 95% confidence interval — the actual value is expected to fall within this range 95% of the time.
  • The forecast is based on historical patterns only. External events (vessel schedule changes, weather disruptions, policy changes) are not predicted.

Opportunity Cost (Blocked Capacity)

Opportunity cost analysis

Overstaying containers (10,658 containers) occupy 17,612 TEU-slots (avg dwell: 13.6 days). At $85/TEU throughput revenue, these blocked slots represent $1,497,020/day in lost capacity — compared to $601,178/day earned in demurrage.

Key insight: The opportunity cost of blocked capacity EXCEEDS demurrage revenue. Prioritize clearance of overstaying containers to unlock throughput capacity.
What is opportunity cost and why it matters
Every TEU-slot occupied by an overstaying container is a slot that cannot serve a new container. If your terminal earns $85 per TEU in handling/throughput revenue, then 17,612 blocked TEU-slots represent $1,497,020 in revenue that could have been earned each day those slots are occupied.

Important caveat: This is a theoretical maximum — it assumes every freed slot would immediately be filled by a new revenue-generating container. In practice, actual recovered revenue depends on your terminal's demand pipeline and vessel call schedule. Typically 40–70% of the theoretical maximum is realizable. Even at 50% realization, the impact is substantial and should inform clearance prioritization.

What-if: throughput revenue sensitivity

If throughput revenue is...Daily opportunity cost becomes...vs demurrage earned
$59/TEU (-30%)$1,047,914/dayExceeds demurrage
$85/TEU (current)$1,497,020/dayExceeds demurrage
$110/TEU (+30%)$1,946,126/dayExceeds demurrage
$128/TEU (+50%)$2,245,530/dayExceeds demurrage
How to use the sensitivity table
The table above shows how the opportunity cost calculation changes if your actual throughput revenue per TEU is different from the configured value. If your terminal serves higher-value cargo or has premium service contracts, the true opportunity cost may be significantly higher. Adjust the avg_throughput_revenue_per_teu value in your tariff configuration to reflect your terminal's actual economics.

8. Business Recommendations

Recommendations are prioritized by segment dwell impact (longest-dwelling first):

Chronic Blockers (2,183 containers, avg 34.4 days): Initiate formal clearance procedures. Assess legal options for abandoned cargo. Relocate to peripheral blocks immediately to free operational capacity. Note: these are predominantly 40ft (2-TEU) containers — each one blocks double the yard capacity of a 20ft unit.
Standard Dwellers (20ft, longest dwell) (6,586 containers, avg 6.2 days): Monitor for drift toward Extended Stay. Send automated pickup reminders at day 3-4.
Standard Dwellers (40ft, mid-range) (5,909 containers, avg 5.9 days): Monitor for drift toward Extended Stay. Send automated pickup reminders at day 3-4. Note: these are predominantly 40ft (2-TEU) containers — each one blocks double the yard capacity of a 20ft unit.
Standard Dwellers (40ft) (5,107 containers, avg 4.8 days): Monitor for drift toward Extended Stay. Send automated pickup reminders at day 3-4. Note: these are predominantly 40ft (2-TEU) containers — each one blocks double the yard capacity of a 20ft unit.
Efficient Operators (40ft, longest dwell) (7,941 containers, avg 2.8 days): Maintain current processes. Consider loyalty incentives for shipping lines with consistently low dwell. Note: these are predominantly 40ft (2-TEU) containers — each one blocks double the yard capacity of a 20ft unit.
Efficient Operators (40ft, mid-range) (6,928 containers, avg 2.6 days): Maintain current processes. Consider loyalty incentives for shipping lines with consistently low dwell. Note: these are predominantly 40ft (2-TEU) containers — each one blocks double the yard capacity of a 20ft unit.
Efficient Operators (20ft) (9,702 containers, avg 2.2 days): Maintain current processes. Consider loyalty incentives for shipping lines with consistently low dwell.
Fast Movers (4,062 containers, avg 0.5 days): Optimize gate scheduling to reduce wait times. Pre-position near berth for transhipment efficiency. Note: these are predominantly 40ft (2-TEU) containers — each one blocks double the yard capacity of a 20ft unit.
Implementation priority
Start with the top segment (longest dwell) as it has the highest financial impact per container. Even small improvements in clearing overstaying containers can unlock significant yard capacity. For example, reducing the overstayer segment's average dwell by just 2 days frees TEU-slots equivalent to adding virtual yard capacity without any infrastructure investment.

How to use this list
  • Contact shipping lines listed in the table — escalate demurrage notices for their specific containers.
  • Check customs status for import containers — many long-dwell imports are stuck in customs clearance, not abandoned.
  • Cross-reference with vessel visits — if multiple containers from the same vessel are overstaying, there may be a systemic issue (incomplete documentation, disputed cargo).
  • Relocate chronic blockers — containers dwelling 30+ days should be moved to peripheral blocks to free prime operating space.
  • For the complete list of all containers with their segment assignments, see dwell_features.csv merged with cluster_labels.csv.

10. Assumptions & Limitations

  • Dwell time is measured only for containers with both gate-in and gate-out timestamps. Containers still in yard are excluded from dwell calculations but included in utilization analysis.
  • Clustering assumes Euclidean distance in standardized feature space. Non-spherical cluster shapes may not be well captured by K-Means.
  • Forecasting models assume historical patterns continue. External disruptions (weather, labor actions, policy changes) are not modelled.
  • Storage cost calculations depend on the accuracy of the tariff configuration provided. Actual invoiced amounts may differ due to negotiated rates and volume discounts.
  • Yard utilization is computed from container records; physical constraints like broken equipment, reserved lanes, or maintenance areas are not modelled.
  • This skill analyzes containerized cargo only. General/breakbulk cargo requires a separate analytics methodology.
  • Future enhancements: BAPLIE integration for predictive yard planning, equipment utilization correlation, real-time gate scheduling via MCP, Port Community System integration for customs clearance tracking.
